title: KeePass vs Proton Pass for Minimalists
meta_description: See why Proton Pass fails first when passwords must be stored inside a vendor-hosted cloud vault rather than a local encrypted database file and why KeePass is the better pick for minimalists.
constraint_lens: Minimalists prefer tools that avoid hosted services and keep password storage entirely under their own control.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want passwords stored entirely on local devices.
one_second_verdict.reason: Proton Pass fails first because passwords must be stored inside a vendor hosted cloud vault.

verdict.summary: KeePass is the better choice for minimalists who refuse to store passwords inside a hosted service. It stores credentials in a local encrypted database file that lives directly on the user's device. Proton Pass requires creating an account and storing the password vault inside the hosted cloud service. For users who want passwords fully under their own control, that cloud dependency adds an unnecessary layer.
verdict.decision_rule: If passwords must be stored inside a vendor-hosted cloud vault rather than a local encrypted database file, Proton Pass fails first.

sections.heading: Why KeePass fits Minimalists better
sections.content: KeePass fits this minimalist because storage control changes more than where the encrypted vault sits. It affects whether the user needs a provider account, how much trust they place in a hosted service during daily use, and how flexibly they can shape backup and portability decisions later. KeePass wins by keeping that control closer to the user.

bullets.point: KeePass keeps the vault under local or user-chosen control
bullets.why_it_matters: Passwords are not forced into a vendor-hosted account model before the user has decided they want that tradeoff.

bullets.point: KeePass reduces dependency on a provider account during daily use
bullets.why_it_matters: The password workflow stays closer to the device or storage path the user already trusts.

bullets.point: KeePass leaves more room to shape backup and storage choices
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when portability and control are part of the reason for choosing the tool.

bullets.point: Proton Pass can still be easier when automatic sync matters more than storage sovereignty
bullets.why_it_matters: A vendor account can reduce setup and daily handling for users who do not want to manage location or backup strategy.

bullets.point: Proton Pass often gives a smoother login experience out of the box
bullets.why_it_matters: Hosted accounts usually pair naturally with browser extensions and cross-device access.

bullets.point: Proton Pass asks for less manual thinking about where the vault lives
bullets.why_it_matters: That can be the better tradeoff when convenience beats local control.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: KeePass becomes the wrong fit when the user would rather offload storage and sync decisions to a hosted account model.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Proton Pass if convenience now matters more than vault ownership.

items.fails_when: Proton Pass breaks down when the user does not want credentials forced into a vendor-hosted account or cloud vault.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose KeePass when local control is the non-negotiable boundary.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the user decides automatic hosted sync is worth more than direct control over vault location and storage choices. Then Proton Pass may make more sense.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose KeePass if vault ownership matters more than hosted convenience.
Choose Proton Pass if you want sync and account management handled for you.
Avoid Proton Pass when provider-controlled vault storage is the exact dealbreaker.

faqs.q: Why does local control matter so much here?
faqs.a: Because it affects trust boundary, daily dependence on a provider account, and how flexibly the vault can be backed up or moved later.

faqs.q: When is Proton Pass still worth choosing?
faqs.a: Proton Pass is still worth choosing when automatic hosted sync is more valuable than storage sovereignty.

faqs.q: What usually makes Proton Pass the wrong fit?
faqs.a: The user does not want the vault tied to a vendor-hosted account or cloud model in the first place.

faqs.q: Does KeePass always mean more work?
faqs.a: Often some, but that tradeoff can be the point when local control is the priority.

related_pages.title: KeeWeb vs Proton Pass for Minimalists
